quality.rationale: Links tidal gravity, quadrupole coupling, and spin precession via local-frame calculation and covariant generalization; requires multi-step tensor algebra and symmetry arguments.

origin.question:
[ć–‡ä»¶ 1: 1_s11_11.8.md]

Problem 11.8. A nonspherical spinning body in an inhomogeneous gravitational field experiences a torque which causes its intrinsic spin 4-vector S to change with time. If $\mathbf{u}$ is the 4-velocity of the center of mass of the object, freely moving along a geodesic, show that
$$
\frac {\mathrm {D S}^{\kappa}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \varepsilon^{\kappa \beta \alpha \mu} u_{\mu} u^{\sigma} u^{\lambda} t_{\beta \eta} R^{\eta}_{\sigma \alpha \lambda}.
$$
Here $\mathfrak{t}\beta \eta$ is the "reduced quadrupole moment tensor" $\mathfrak{t}^{\mathrm{ij}} = \int \rho (\mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{i}}\mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{j}} - \frac{1}{3}\mathbf{r}^{2}\delta^{\mathrm{ij}})\mathbf{d}^{3}\mathbf{x}$ in the rest frame of the center of mass, $\mathfrak{t}^{\alpha \beta}\mathfrak{u}_{\alpha} = 0$ and the Riemann tensor is generated externally to the body in question and is assumed to be approximately constant over the body.
origin.answer: $\\frac {\\mathrm {D S}^{\\kappa}}{\\mathrm {d} \\tau} = \\varepsilon^{\\kappa \\beta \\alpha \\mu} u_{\\mu} u^{\\sigma} u^{\\lambda} t_{\\beta \\eta} R^{\\eta}_{\\sigma \\alpha \\lambda}$
origin.solution:
Solution 11.8. Do the computation in the local comoving Lorentz frame of the center of mass of the body. Treating the center of mass as the reference point in the equation of geodesic deviation, the relative acceleration of a mass element at position $\mathbf{x}^{\hat{\mathrm{j}}}$ is
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \mathbf {x}^{\hat {\mathrm{j}}}}{\mathrm {d} t^{2}} = - R^{\hat {\mathrm{j}}}_{\hat {\mathrm{0 k}}} \hat {\mathrm{0}}^{\mathbf{x}^{\hat {\mathrm{k}}}}. \tag {1}
$$
The $i^{\text{th}}$ component of the torque per unit volume is then $-\varepsilon_{\hat{i}1\hat{j}}\mathbf{x}^{\hat{l}}\rho \mathbf{R}^{\hat{j}}\hat{0}\hat{k}\hat{0}^{\hat{x}^{\hat{k}}}$ , where $ho$ is the mass density at $\mathbf{x}^{\hat{j}}$ . The total torque, which is just the time derivative of the intrinsic angular momentum, is
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S}_{\hat {\mathbf{i}}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \varepsilon_{\hat {\mathbf{i}} \hat {\mathbf{1}} \hat {\mathbf{j}}} \mathbf{R}^{\hat {\mathbf{j}}} \hat {\mathbf{0}} \hat {\mathbf{k}} \hat {\mathbf{0}} \int \rho \mathbf {x}^{\hat {\mathbf{i}}} \mathbf {x}^{\hat {\mathbf{k}}} \mathrm {d}^{3} \mathbf{x} \tag {2}
$$
if we approximate $\mathbb{R}_{\hat{0}\hat{k}\hat{0}}^{\hat{j}}$ as constant over the body. Because of the symmetry properties of $\varepsilon_{\hat{i}\hat{j}}\hat{R}_{\hat{0}\hat{k}\hat{0}}^{\hat{j}}$ , Equation (2) is equivalent to
$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} S_{\widehat {\mathbf{j}}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \varepsilon_{\widehat {\mathbf{i}} \widehat {\mathbf{j}}} t_{\widehat {\mathbf{1} k}} R_{\widehat {\mathbf{j}} \widehat {\mathbf{0}} \widehat {\mathbf{k}} \widehat {\mathbf{0}}}, \tag {3}
$$
where
$$
t_{\widehat {1} \widehat {k}} \equiv \int \rho (x^{\widehat {1}} x^{\widehat {k}} - \frac {1}{3} r^{2} \delta^{\widehat {1} \widehat {k}}) d^{3} x.
$$
If one now defines the quadrupole tensor, $t^{\alpha \beta}$ to be such that $t^{\alpha \beta} u_{\beta} = 0$ i.e. to have only spatial components in a comoving local frame, then the invariant tensor expression of Equation (3) is
$$
\frac {\mathrm {D S}^{\kappa}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \varepsilon^{\kappa \beta a \mu} \mathrm{u}_{\mu} \mathrm{u}^{\sigma} \mathrm{u}^{\lambda} \mathrm{t} \beta \eta \mathrm{R}^{\eta} \sigma a \lambda . \tag {4}
$$
Note that either in the case of a spherical body, or a body small enough so that Riemann $\times$ (size of body)$^2 \approx 0$ , Equation (4) reduces to $\mathrm{DS}^K / \mathrm{d}\tau = 0$ . (Cf. Problem 11.4 with $a = 0$ .)
